summary: Spolsky reflects on the dot-com bust and pivots to discussing 'eating your own dog food' — the practice of using your own software product. After the euphoria and schadenfreude of watching inflated startups fail, he describes how taking a build of CityDesk home and actually trying to use it revealed 45 bugs in a single afternoon, including showstoppers that formal testing had missed. He contrasts this with Juno Online Services, where employees used Microsoft Outlook instead of their own email product, leading to a terrible user experience that couldn't compete with AOL. The post argues that dogfooding is essential for building good software and that sustainable, customer-focused businesses will outlast the venture-capital-fueled chaos of the dot-com era.
key_points: Formal QA testing (pulling down every menu) missed critical showstoppers that two hours of actual product usage immediately revealed — 45 bugs in one Sunday afternoon.
Dogfooding not only finds bugs but inspires productivity improvements: Spolsky wrote an automatic page-import subroutine that reduced a full day's work to half a minute.
Juno Online Services failed to eat its own dog food — 175 of 178 employees used Outlook instead of Juno — which meant the product's poor experience went unfixed while AOL gained market share.
The dot-com bust, while initially entertaining as schadenfreude, revealed that 'Get Big Fast' without sustainable business fundamentals was never a real strategy.
In the post-dot-com economy, the path forward is listening to customers, using your own product, and focusing on staying in business rather than chasing venture capital.
money_quotes: Five hundred 31-year-olds in Dockers discovered that just copying Jeff Bezos wasn't a business plan.
All the testing we did, meticulously pulling down every menu and seeing if it worked right, didn't uncover the showstoppers that made it impossible to do what the product was intended to allow.
Trying to use the product, as a customer would, found these showstoppers in a minute.
Hey, I'm a programmer! In the time it took me to import one page and adjust the pictures, I could write a subroutine that does it automatically!

Sometimes you download software and you just can't believe how bad it is, or how hard it is to accomplish the very simple tasks that the software tries to accomplish. Chances are, it's because the developers of the software don't use it.
The president was very insistent that we show popup ads at six different points in time, until he got home and got six popup ads, and said, 'You know what? Maybe just two popups.'
We didn't eat our own dog food, because it was disgusting, and management was sufficiently dysfunctional that we simply were not allowed to fix it, and at least make it tolerable to eat.
key_insight: Using your own product as a real customer would is the fastest and most effective way to find critical usability problems that no amount of formal testing will uncover.
